TARGET   := nephilim.kernel
ISO      := nephilim.iso
ISODIR   := isodir

CC       := clang
LD       := ld.lld
NASM     := nasm
PYTHON   := $(shell which python 2>/dev/null || which python3 2>/dev/null)

INCLUDES := -Iinclude

# Kernel flags: target is x86-64 — SSE/AVX available and enabled
CFLAGS   := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP
LDFLAGS  := -m elf_x86_64 -nostdlib -T linker.ld \
            --no-dynamic-linker -z max-page-size=0x1000

# Userland flags
UCFLAGS  := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP

# MuJS flags — relax warnings for third-party code
JSCFLAGS := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -Iexternal/include/mujs \
            -Wno-unused-function -Wno-unused-variable \
            -Wno-sign-compare -Wno-implicit-fallthrough \
            -Wno-missing-field-initializers

# ── Kernel objects ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KOBJS    := boot/boot.o boot/gdt_flush.o boot/isr_stubs.o boot/ctx_switch.o \
            boot/font8x16.o \
            drivers/keyboard.o drivers/serial.o \
            kernel/kernel.o kernel/vga.o kernel/gop.o kernel/detect.o \
            kernel/gdt.o kernel/idt.o kernel/syscall.o kernel/process.o \
            kernel/timer.o kernel/pmm.o kernel/vmm.o \
            kernel/js_runtime.o kernel/seed.o kernel/minit.o \
            fs/vfs.o fs/rawfs.o fs/ext2.o \
            lib/libtranslateunix.o \
            stdlib/string.o stdlib/stdlib.o stdlib/malloc.o \
            stdlib/math.o stdlib/printf.o stdlib/setjmp.o stdlib/time.o \
            userland/shell/main.o \
            userland/snake/main.o \
            userland/apps.o

# Auto-generated dependency files (produced by -MMD -MP)
-include $(KOBJS:.o=.d)

.PHONY: all iso clean

all: $(TARGET)

# ── JS source → C string-literal header ──────────────────────────────────────
JS_HEADERS := \
    userland/signal.js.h \
    userland/calculator/main.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/ls.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/cat.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/touch.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/mkdir.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/rm.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/stat.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/write.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/wc.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/uname.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/cd.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/ps.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/cp.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/mv.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/grep.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/sed.js.h \
    userland/quill/main.js.h

userland/signal.js.h: userland/signal.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

userland/calculator/main.js.h: userland/calculator/main.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

userland/coreutils/%.js.h: userland/coreutils/%.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

userland/quill/main.js.h: userland/quill/main.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

# ── Assembly ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
boot/boot.o: boot/boot.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

boot/gdt_flush.o: boot/gdt_flush.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

boot/isr_stubs.o: boot/isr_stubs.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

boot/ctx_switch.o: boot/ctx_switch.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

boot/font8x16.o: boot/font8x16.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

# ── Kernel C ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
kernel/kernel.o: kernel/kernel.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/vga.o: kernel/vga.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/gop.o: kernel/gop.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/detect.o: kernel/detect.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/gdt.o: kernel/gdt.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/idt.o: kernel/idt.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/syscall.o: kernel/syscall.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/process.o: kernel/process.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/timer.o: kernel/timer.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/pmm.o: kernel/pmm.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/vmm.o: kernel/vmm.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# MuJS runtime — compiled with relaxed flags, port header injected first
kernel/js_runtime.o: kernel/js_runtime.c userland/signal.js.h
	$(CC) $(JSCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/seed.o: kernel/seed.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/minit.o: kernel/minit.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Filesystem ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fs/vfs.o: fs/vfs.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

fs/rawfs.o: fs/rawfs.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

fs/ext2.o: fs/ext2.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Lib ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
lib/libtranslateunix.o: lib/libtranslateunix.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Drivers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drivers/keyboard.o: drivers/keyboard.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

drivers/serial.o: drivers/serial.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Stdlib ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
stdlib/string.o: stdlib/string.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/stdlib.o: stdlib/stdlib.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/malloc.o: stdlib/malloc.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/math.o: stdlib/math.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/printf.o: stdlib/printf.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/setjmp.o: stdlib/setjmp.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

stdlib/time.o: stdlib/time.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Userland ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
userland/shell/main.o: userland/shell/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/snake/main.o: userland/snake/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/apps.o: userland/apps.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c userland/apps.c -o $@

# ── Final kernel link ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$(TARGET): $(KOBJS)
	$(LD) $(LDFLAGS) $(KOBJS) -o $@

# ── ISO ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GRUB_MKRESCUE := $(shell which grub2-mkrescue 2>/dev/null || which grub-mkrescue 2>/dev/null)

# grub-mkrescue flags:
#   --modules includes both multiboot (MB1) and multiboot2 (MB2/UEFI) modules
#   plus efi_gop so GRUB can negotiate a GOP framebuffer on UEFI firmware.
GRUB_MODULES := multiboot multiboot2 normal iso9660 gfxterm

iso: $(TARGET)
	mkdir -p $(ISODIR)/boot/grub
	cp $(TARGET) $(ISODIR)/boot/$(TARGET)
	cp grub/grub.cfg $(ISODIR)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
	$(GRUB_MKRESCUE) --modules="$(GRUB_MODULES)" -o $(ISO) $(ISODIR)

# ── Run ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QEMU    := $(shell which qemu-system-x86_64 2>/dev/null)
# OVMF firmware paths — openSUSE ships split code+vars images in qemu-ovmf-x86_64.
# Override on the command line if your distro puts them elsewhere:
#   make run-uefi OVMF_CODE=/path/to/code.fd OVMF_VARS=/path/to/vars.fd
OVMF_CODE ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_CODE.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_CODE.fd))
OVMF_VARS ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_VARS.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_VARS.fd))

# BIOS legacy run (Multiboot 1 fallback menu entry)
run: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown

# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS)

# ── Clean ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET) $(ISO)
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.o' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.d' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	rm -f $(JS_HEADERS)
	rm -rf $(ISODIR) external/include/mujs/*.o

aiTitle: Upgrade code to Multiboot 2 for UEFI and GOP graphics

content.text: However, the vars file is read-write — UEFI will try to modify it (to save boot entries etc.). You shouldn't let QEMU write to the system-installed file. Update `run-uefi` to copy vars to a temp file first:

input.old_string:
# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS)
input.new_string:
# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer.
# Vars are copied to a temp file so QEMU never writes back to the system image.
OVMF_VARS_TMP := /tmp/nephilim-ovmf-vars.bin
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	cp $(OVMF_VARS) $(OVMF_VARS_TMP)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS_TMP)

	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

userland/calculator/main.js.h: userland/calculator/main.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

userland/coreutils/%.js.h: userland/coreutils/%.js
	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

	$(PYTHON) tools/js2ch.py $< > $@

# ── Assembly ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

# ── Kernel C ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/vga.o: kernel/vga.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/gop.o: kernel/gop.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/detect.o: kernel/detect.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/gdt.o: kernel/gdt.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/idt.o: kernel/idt.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/syscall.o: kernel/syscall.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/process.o: kernel/process.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/timer.o: kernel/timer.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/pmm.o: kernel/pmm.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/vmm.o: kernel/vmm.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# MuJS runtime — compiled with relaxed flags, port header injected first
kernel/js_runtime.o: kernel/js_runtime.c userland/signal.js.h
	$(CC) $(JSCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/seed.o: kernel/seed.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

kernel/minit.o: kernel/minit.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Filesystem ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fs/vfs.o: fs/vfs.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

fs/rawfs.o: fs/rawfs.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

fs/ext2.o: fs/ext2.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Lib ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
lib/libtranslateunix.o: lib/libtranslateunix.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Drivers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drivers/keyboard.o: drivers/keyboard.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

drivers/serial.o: drivers/serial.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Stdlib ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
stdlib/string.o: stdlib/string.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/stdlib.o: stdlib/stdlib.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/malloc.o: stdlib/malloc.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/math.o: stdlib/math.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/printf.o: stdlib/printf.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/setjmp.o: stdlib/setjmp.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

stdlib/time.o: stdlib/time.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Userland ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
userland/shell/main.o: userland/shell/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/snake/main.o: userland/snake/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/apps.o: userland/apps.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c userland/apps.c -o $@

# ── Final kernel link ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$(TARGET): $(KOBJS)
	$(LD) $(LDFLAGS) $(KOBJS) -o $@

# ── ISO ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GRUB_MKRESCUE := $(shell which grub2-mkrescue 2>/dev/null || which grub-mkrescue 2>/dev/null)

# grub-mkrescue flags:
#   --modules includes both multiboot (MB1) and multiboot2 (MB2/UEFI) modules
#   plus efi_gop so GRUB can negotiate a GOP framebuffer on UEFI firmware.
GRUB_MODULES := multiboot multiboot2 normal iso9660 gfxterm

iso: $(TARGET)
	mkdir -p $(ISODIR)/boot/grub
	cp $(TARGET) $(ISODIR)/boot/$(TARGET)
	cp grub/grub.cfg $(ISODIR)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
	$(GRUB_MKRESCUE) --modules="$(GRUB_MODULES)" -o $(ISO) $(ISODIR)

# ── Run ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QEMU    := $(shell which qemu-system-x86_64 2>/dev/null)
# OVMF firmware paths — openSUSE ships split code+vars images in qemu-ovmf-x86_64.
# Override on the command line if your distro puts them elsewhere:
#   make run-uefi OVMF_CODE=/path/to/code.fd OVMF_VARS=/path/to/vars.fd
OVMF_CODE ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_CODE.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_CODE.fd))
OVMF_VARS ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_VARS.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_VARS.fd))

# BIOS legacy run (Multiboot 1 fallback menu entry)
run: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown

# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS)

# ── Clean ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET) $(ISO)
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.o' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.d' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	rm -f $(JS_HEADERS)
	rm -rf $(ISODIR) external/include/mujs/*.o

lines: -# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer
+# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer.
+# Vars are copied to a temp file so QEMU never writes back to the system image.

content.text:
The Makefile already has the updated wildcards but you're running an old cached build. Do `make iso` first to rebuild, then run-uefi:

```bash
make iso && make run-uefi
```

The `wildcard` function in Make only evaluates at parse time — since the Makefile changed after the last `make iso`, you need a fresh run. The paths `/usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin` and `ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin` are now in the wildcard list and will be found correctly.

lib/libtranslateunix.o: lib/libtranslateunix.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Drivers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drivers/keyboard.o: drivers/keyboard.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

drivers/serial.o: drivers/serial.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Stdlib ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
stdlib/string.o: stdlib/string.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/stdlib.o: stdlib/stdlib.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/malloc.o: stdlib/malloc.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/math.o: stdlib/math.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/printf.o: stdlib/printf.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/setjmp.o: stdlib/setjmp.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

stdlib/time.o: stdlib/time.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Userland ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
userland/shell/main.o: userland/shell/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/snake/main.o: userland/snake/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/apps.o: userland/apps.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c userland/apps.c -o $@

# ── Final kernel link ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$(TARGET): $(KOBJS)
	$(LD) $(LDFLAGS) $(KOBJS) -o $@

# ── ISO ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GRUB_MKRESCUE := $(shell which grub2-mkrescue 2>/dev/null || which grub-mkrescue 2>/dev/null)

# grub-mkrescue flags:
#   --modules includes both multiboot (MB1) and multiboot2 (MB2/UEFI) modules
#   plus efi_gop so GRUB can negotiate a GOP framebuffer on UEFI firmware.
GRUB_MODULES := multiboot multiboot2 normal iso9660 gfxterm

iso: $(TARGET)
	mkdir -p $(ISODIR)/boot/grub
	cp $(TARGET) $(ISODIR)/boot/$(TARGET)
	cp grub/grub.cfg $(ISODIR)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
	$(GRUB_MKRESCUE) --modules="$(GRUB_MODULES)" -o $(ISO) $(ISODIR)

# ── Run ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QEMU    := $(shell which qemu-system-x86_64 2>/dev/null)
# OVMF firmware paths — openSUSE ships split code+vars images in qemu-ovmf-x86_64.
# Override on the command line if your distro puts them elsewhere:
#   make run-uefi OVMF_CODE=/path/to/code.fd OVMF_VARS=/path/to/vars.fd
OVMF_CODE ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_CODE.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_CODE.fd))
OVMF_VARS ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_VARS.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_VARS.fd))

# BIOS legacy run (Multiboot 1 fallback menu entry)
run: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown

# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer.
# Vars are copied to a temp file so QEMU never writes back to the system image.
OVMF_VARS_TMP := /tmp/nephilim-ovmf-vars.bin
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	cp $(OVMF_VARS) $(OVMF_VARS_TMP)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS_TMP)

# ── Clean ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET) $(ISO)
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.o' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.d' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	rm -f $(JS_HEADERS)
	rm -rf $(ISODIR) external/include/mujs/*.o

content.text:
grub.cfg is fine — no `gfxterm` there. The error was solely from the `gfxterm` module being loaded (it tries to init a video mode on load). Now rebuild the ISO:

```bash
make iso && make run-uefi
```

GRUB will use its plain text terminal instead, which works fine on both BIOS and UEFI. Once GRUB hands off to the kernel, the kernel's own GOP driver takes over for the framebuffer — GRUB's video mode is irrelevant at that point.

still same error
Here are the docs as per OSDev

GOP
Jump to navigation
Jump to search

This is the new standard for UEFI that superseded VESA (BIOS) and UGA (EFI 1.0).
Contents

    1 Graphics Output Protocol
        1.1 Detecting GOP
        1.2 Get the Current Mode
        1.3 Query Available Video Modes
        1.4 Set Video Mode and Get the Framebuffer
        1.5 Plotting Pixels
        1.6 Don't Read From Video Memory
    2 Acceleration
        2.1 Modes
    3 See Also
        3.1 Articles
        3.2 External Links

Graphics Output Protocol

It has basically the same functions as VESA, you can query the modes, set the modes. It also provides an efficient BitBlitter function, which you can't use from your OS unfortunately. GOP is an EFI Boot Time Service, meaning you can't access it after you call ExitBootServices(). However, the framebuffer provided by GOP persists, so you can continue to use it for graphics output in your OS.

NOTE: UEFI uses its own ABI. You can either configure your build environment to use that globally, or you must use a wrapper function. These examples use the latter for compatibility reasons. Omit uefi_call_wrapper if you have configured your build system for the former. See GNU-EFI for more information.
Detecting GOP

As with other UEFI protocols, you have to locate a structure with the function pointers first using the protocol's GUID.

  EFI_GUID gopGuid = EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_PROTOCOL_GUID;
  EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_PROTOCOL *gop;

  status = uefi_call_wrapper(BS->LocateProtocol, 3, &gopGuid, NULL, (void**)&gop);
  if(EFI_ERROR(status))
    PrintLn(L"Unable to locate GOP");

GOP is the default protocol, so you should be able to locate it on all UEFI firmware. It can probably only fail if you're on an old EFI (pre-UEFI) machine, like an Itanium-based computer or a Mac released before 2009.

If your kernel uses GRUB, you need to insert a module called "all_video" before loading the kernel to add UEFI GOP compatibility. Not doing so will display a message saying "WARNING: no console will be available to OS".

insmod all_video

menuentry "Example OS" {
  multiboot2  /boot/kernel.bin
  boot
}

Get the Current Mode

In order to get the mode code for the current video mode, you must set the mode as well to circumvent some buggy UEFI firmware. Otherwise this is done using the QueryMode function, and then gop->Mode->Mode will contain the code.

  EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_MODE_INFORMATION *info;
  UINTN SizeOfInfo, numModes, nativeMode;

  status = uefi_call_wrapper(gop->QueryMode, 4, gop, gop->Mode==NULL?0:gop->Mode->Mode, &SizeOfInfo, &info);
  // this is needed to get the current video mode
  if (status == EFI_NOT_STARTED)
    status = uefi_call_wrapper(gop->SetMode, 2, gop, 0);
  if(EFI_ERROR(status)) {
    PrintLn(L"Unable to get native mode");
  } else {
    nativeMode = gop->Mode->Mode;
    numModes = gop->Mode->MaxMode;
  }

Query Available Video Modes

Similarly to VESA, there's no standard mode codes, rather you have a function to query the available modes. Now you know how many modes there are (numModes above), and which one is currently set (nativeMode). You can iterate on the modes and query the information structure for each:

for (i = 0; i < numModes; i++) {
  status = uefi_call_wrapper(gop->QueryMode, 4, gop, i, &SizeOfInfo, &info);
  PrintLn(L"mode %03d width %d height %d format %x%s",
    i,
    info->HorizontalResolution,
    info->VerticalResolution,
    info->PixelFormat,
    i == nativeMode ? "(current)" : ""
  );
}

Set Video Mode and Get the Framebuffer

This is pretty easy. The mode argument is between 0 and numModes.

  status = uefi_call_wrapper(gop->SetMode, 2, gop, mode);
  if(EFI_ERROR(status)) {
    PrintLn(L"Unable to set mode %03d", mode);
  } else {
    // get framebuffer
    PrintLn(L"Framebuffer address %x size %d, width %d height %d pixelsperline %d",
      gop->Mode->FrameBufferBase,
      gop->Mode->FrameBufferSize,
      gop->Mode->Info->HorizontalResolution,
      gop->Mode->Info->VerticalResolution,
      gop->Mode->Info->PixelsPerScanLine
    );
  }

To get the same value as scanline in VESA (also commonly called pitch in many graphics libraries), you have to multiply PixelsPerScanLine by the number of bytes per pixel. That can be detected by examining the gop->Mode->Info->PixelFormat field. For example with 32 bit packed pixel formats,

  pitch = 4 * gop->Mode->Info->PixelsPerScanLine;

Plotting Pixels

    Main article: Drawing In Protected Mode

Now you can use the returned framebuffer exactly the same way as you would with VESA, there's absolutely no difference. To calculate the offset for an (X,Y) coordinate on screen, do pitch*Y+pixelbytes*X. For example for 32 bit true-color (where pixelbytes is 4):

static inline void PlotPixel_32bpp(int x, int y, uint32_t pixel)
{
   *((uint32_t*)(gop->Mode->FrameBufferBase + 4 * gop->Mode->Info->PixelsPerScanLine * y + 4 * x)) = pixel;
}

For drawing characters, you can use the same method described in VGA Fonts.
Don't Read From Video Memory

Reading from the video memory is very slow. Use double buffering instead.
Acceleration

EFI GOP provides some rudimentary accelerated 2D Graphics functions. These are only available as BootServices, so once you call ExitBootServices(), you need to use the Framebuffer directly (easy, but slow), or load your own graphics card driver (fast, but hard).

An alternative approach might be to frankenstein the GOP driver (which typically can be found in a BAR on the graphics adapter, or you can query UEFI with LocateProtocol and then use IMAGE_LOADED_PROTOCOL to find the image. This would be a massive hack, though, for some fairly rudimentary display services.

Acceleration is provided through one function, Block Transfer. It has several modes.

typedef
EFI_STATUS
(EFIAPI *EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_PROTOCOL_BLT) (
  IN  EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_PROTOCOL            *This,
  IN  EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_BLT_PIXEL           *BltBuffer,   OPTIONAL
  IN  EFI_GRAPHICS_OUTPUT_BLT_OPERATION       BltOperation,
  IN  UINTN                                   SourceX,
  IN  UINTN                                   SourceY,
  IN  UINTN                                   DestinationX,
  IN  UINTN                                   DestinationY,
  IN  UINTN                                   Width,
  IN  UINTN                                   Height,
  IN  UINTN                                   Delta         OPTIONAL
  );

Modes
Name 	Description
EfiBltVideoFill 	Fills the specified area of the screen with the pixel value in the BltBuffer at (0, 0)
EfiBltVideoToBltBuffer 	Copies the specified source rectangle from the display to the BltBuffer at (Dx, Dy)
EfiBltBufferToVideo 	Copies from the BltBuffer to the video display at (Dx, Dy)
EfiBltVideoToVideo 	Copies from one rectangle of the display to another.

Delta should be set to the length of a line of the BltBuffer when using it in a copy mode, although if the (X,Y) are zero and delta is zero, the GOP will assume that the Width parameter is the length of a line.
See Also
Articles

    VESA - the former video standard

External Links

    GOP description in the UEFI Specification
    GOP at Wikipedia
    UEFI Graphic Features at Wikipedia
    EDK2 on implementing GOP
    Intel documentation on GOP

TARGET   := nephilim.kernel
ISO      := nephilim.iso
ISODIR   := isodir

CC       := clang
LD       := ld.lld
NASM     := nasm
PYTHON   := $(shell which python 2>/dev/null || which python3 2>/dev/null)

INCLUDES := -Iinclude

# Kernel flags: target is x86-64 — SSE/AVX available and enabled
CFLAGS   := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP
LDFLAGS  := -m elf_x86_64 -nostdlib -T linker.ld \
            --no-dynamic-linker -z max-page-size=0x1000

# Userland flags
UCFLAGS  := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP

# MuJS flags — relax warnings for third-party code
JSCFLAGS := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -Iexternal/include/mujs \
            -Wno-unused-function -Wno-unused-variable \
            -Wno-sign-compare -Wno-implicit-fallthrough \
            -Wno-missing-field-initializers

# ── Kernel objects ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KOBJS    := boot/boot.o boot/gdt_flush.o boot/isr_stubs.o boot/ctx_switch.o \
            boot/font8x16.o \
            drivers/keyboard.o drivers/serial.o \
            kernel/kernel.o kernel/vga.o kernel/gop.o kernel/detect.o \
            kernel/gdt.o kernel/idt.o kernel/syscall.o kernel/process.o \
            kernel/timer.o kernel/pmm.o kernel/vmm.o \
            kernel/js_runtime.o kernel/seed.o kernel/minit.o \
            fs/vfs.o fs/rawfs.o fs/ext2.o \
            lib/libtranslateunix.o \
            stdlib/string.o stdlib/stdlib.o stdlib/malloc.o \
            stdlib/math.o stdlib/printf.o stdlib/setjmp.o stdlib/time.o \
            userland/shell/main.o \
            userland/snake/main.o \
            userland/apps.o

# Auto-generated dependency files (produced by -MMD -MP)
-include $(KOBJS:.o=.d)

.PHONY: all iso clean

all: $(TARGET)

# ── JS source → C string-literal header ──────────────────────────────────────

    userland/signal.js.h \
    userland/calculator/main.js.h \

    userland/coreutils/cat.js.h \

# ── Filesystem ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# ── Lib ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Drivers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
drivers/keyboard.o: drivers/keyboard.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

drivers/serial.o: drivers/serial.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Stdlib ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
stdlib/string.o: stdlib/string.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/stdlib.o: stdlib/stdlib.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/malloc.o: stdlib/malloc.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/math.o: stdlib/math.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/printf.o: stdlib/printf.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

stdlib/setjmp.o: stdlib/setjmp.asm
	$(NASM) -f elf64 $< -o $@

stdlib/time.o: stdlib/time.c
	$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

# ── Userland ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
userland/shell/main.o: userland/shell/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/snake/main.o: userland/snake/main.c
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c $< -o $@

userland/apps.o: userland/apps.c $(JS_HEADERS)
	$(CC) $(UCFLAGS) -c userland/apps.c -o $@

# ── Final kernel link ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
$(TARGET): $(KOBJS)
	$(LD) $(LDFLAGS) $(KOBJS) -o $@

# ── ISO ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
GRUB_MKRESCUE := $(shell which grub2-mkrescue 2>/dev/null || which grub-mkrescue 2>/dev/null)

# grub-mkrescue flags:
#   --modules includes both multiboot (MB1) and multiboot2 (MB2/UEFI) modules
#   plus efi_gop so GRUB can negotiate a GOP framebuffer on UEFI firmware.
GRUB_MODULES := multiboot multiboot2 normal iso9660

iso: $(TARGET)
	mkdir -p $(ISODIR)/boot/grub
	cp $(TARGET) $(ISODIR)/boot/$(TARGET)
	cp grub/grub.cfg $(ISODIR)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
	$(GRUB_MKRESCUE) --modules="$(GRUB_MODULES)" -o $(ISO) $(ISODIR)

# ── Run ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QEMU    := $(shell which qemu-system-x86_64 2>/dev/null)
# OVMF firmware paths — openSUSE ships split code+vars images in qemu-ovmf-x86_64.
# Override on the command line if your distro puts them elsewhere:
#   make run-uefi OVMF_CODE=/path/to/code.fd OVMF_VARS=/path/to/vars.fd
OVMF_CODE ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-code.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_CODE.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_CODE.fd))
OVMF_VARS ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-vars.bin) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/edk2/ovmf/OVMF_VARS.fd) \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/OVMF/OVMF_VARS.fd))

# BIOS legacy run (Multiboot 1 fallback menu entry)
run: $(ISO)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown

# UEFI run — split OVMF code+vars images; boots via Multiboot 2 + GOP framebuffer.
# Vars are copied to a temp file so QEMU never writes back to the system image.
OVMF_VARS_TMP := /tmp/nephilim-ovmf-vars.bin
run-uefi: $(ISO)
	cp $(OVMF_VARS) $(OVMF_VARS_TMP)
	$(QEMU) -cdrom $(ISO) -m 256M -serial stdio -no-reboot -no-shutdown \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,readonly=on,file=$(OVMF_CODE) \
	        -drive if=pflash,format=raw,file=$(OVMF_VARS_TMP)

# ── Clean ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
clean:
	rm -f $(TARGET) $(ISO)
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.o' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	find boot kernel stdlib userland drivers fs lib -name '*.d' -delete 2>/dev/null || true
	rm -f $(JS_HEADERS)
	rm -rf $(ISODIR) external/include/mujs/*.o

make iso && make run-uefi

`all_video` is a meta-module that pulls in every available video backend (including the UEFI GOP driver) so GRUB can negotiate a framebuffer before handing the MB2 framebuffer tag to your kernel.

aiTitle: Upgrade code to Multiboot 2 for UEFI and GOP graphics

content.text:
Two separate problems:

1. **MB2 black screen** — GRUB negotiated a GOP framebuffer but then handed off; the kernel's GOP renderer is likely drawing white-on-black at address 0 because the framebuffer tag isn't being found correctly.
2. **MB1 "no console"** — GRUB is trying to use `all_video` on the BIOS path too, which breaks the legacy text console.

Fix both: scope `insmod all_video` to the MB2 entry only (already done), and add `terminal_output console` to the MB1 entry to restore its text console. For MB2, add `set gfxpayload=keep` which tells GRUB to preserve the GOP framebuffer it negotiated and pass it to the kernel via the MB2 framebuffer tag.

    insmod all_video
    multiboot2 /boot/nephilim.kernel
    boot
}

# ── Legacy fallback: Multiboot 1 (BIOS VGA text mode) ──────────────────────────
menuentry "NephilimOS (Multiboot 1 / BIOS legacy)" {
    multiboot /boot/nephilim.kernel
    boot
}

}

content.text:
LSP false positives as before. Rebuild and run:

```bash
make iso && make run-uefi
```

Watch the terminal (that's the serial output from `-serial stdio`). You'll see one of:

- `[BOOT] no framebuffer tag found` → GRUB isn't passing a framebuffer tag; `gfxpayload=keep` fix should resolve this after the rebuild
- `[BOOT] GOP init FAILED` → tag is present but wrong type/bpp; we'll fix the tag parsing
- `[BOOT] GOP init OK` then blank screen → the framebuffer address itself is wrong

aiTitle: Upgrade code to Multiboot 2 for UEFI and GOP graphics

content.text: `type=1` is the problem — that means **indexed colour** (palette-based), not direct RGB. Our `gop_init_from_mb2_tag` only accepts `type=2` (direct colour). But `bpp=32` with `type=1` on QEMU's virtio-gpu actually means it's an EFI BGR/BGRX direct-colour buffer mislabelled as indexed. Accept type 1 with 32 bpp and hardcode the standard BGRX layout (blue at bit 0, green at 8, red at 16):

content.content: The file d:\NephilimOS\kernel\gop.c has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -    /* We need at least 15 bpp to do anything useful; we asked for 32 */
+        /* Direct colour — channel layout provided by the tag */
+        /* type=1 (indexed) or type=0 (EFI internal) with ≥15 bpp:
+         * QEMU virtio-gpu / OVMF reports type=1 but the buffer is

content.text: Also we need to make sure the framebuffer at `0x80000000` is actually mapped — our page tables only identity-map the first 1 GiB (0x00000000–0x3FFFFFFF), but the framebuffer is at `0x80000000` (2 GiB). Fix `boot.asm` to map 4 GiB instead of 1 GiB:

input.old_string:
align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT
p2_table:   resb 4096   ; PD  (2 MiB pages)
input.new_string:
align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT  (covers 0–512 GiB, one entry per GiB)
p2_table_0: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 0   (0x00000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF)
p2_table_1: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 1   (0x40000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF)
p2_table_2: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 2   (0x80000000 – 0xBFFFFFFF)
p2_table_3: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 3   (0xC0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF)

content.content: The file d:\NephilimOS\boot\boot.asm has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.oldString:
align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT
p2_table:   resb 4096   ; PD  (2 MiB pages)
toolUseResult.newString:
align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT  (covers 0–512 GiB, one entry per GiB)
p2_table_0: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 0   (0x00000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF)
p2_table_1: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 1   (0x40000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF)
p2_table_2: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 2   (0x80000000 – 0xBFFFFFFF)
p2_table_3: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 3   (0xC0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF)
toolUseResult.originalFile:
; ─── Multiboot 2 header ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
;
; Must appear within the first 32 KiB of the OS image, 64-bit aligned.
; On MB2 entry: EAX = 0x36D76289, EBX = physical address of MB2 info struct.
;
MB2_MAGIC       equ 0xE85250D6
MB2_ARCH        equ 0               ; i386 protected mode
MB2_ENTRY_MAGIC equ 0x36D76289

section .mb2hdr
align 8
mb2_header_start:
    dd MB2_MAGIC
    dd MB2_ARCH
    dd (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start)
    dd -(MB2_MAGIC + MB2_ARCH + (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start))

    ; ── Tag: Framebuffer request (type 5) ─────────────────────────────────────
    ; flags=1 → optional (fall back gracefully if firmware can't satisfy it).
    ; width=0 height=0 → no resolution preference; depth=32 → 32 bpp BGRA/BGRX.
    align 8
    dw 5            ; type  = framebuffer
    dw 1            ; flags = optional
    dd 20           ; size  = 20 bytes
    dd 0            ; preferred width  (0 = don't care)
    dd 0            ; preferred height (0 = don't care)
    dd 32           ; preferred depth  (32 bpp)

    ; ── End tag ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    align 8
    dw 0            ; type  = end
    dw 0            ; flags
    dd 8            ; size
mb2_header_end:

; ─── Multiboot 1 header (legacy fallback) ──────────────────────────────────────
;
; Placed immediately after the MB2 header so GRUB (MB1) can still find it within
; the first 8 KiB.  On MB1 entry: EAX = 0x2BADB002, EBX = MB1 info phys addr.
;
MB1_ALIGN     equ 1 << 0
MB1_MEMINFO   equ 1 << 1
MB1_FLAGS     equ MB1_ALIGN | MB1_MEMINFO
MB1_MAGIC     equ 0x1BADB002
MB1_CHECKSUM  equ -(MB1_MAGIC + MB1_FLAGS)

section .mb2hdr          ; same section — keeps both headers contiguous and early
align 4
    dd MB1_MAGIC
    dd MB1_FLAGS
    dd MB1_CHECKSUM

; ─── Stacks + page tables (BSS) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .bss
align 16
stack_bottom:
    resb 16384          ; 16 KiB stack
stack_top:

align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT
p2_table:   resb 4096   ; PD  (2 MiB pages)

; ─── GDT for long mode ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .rodata
gdt64:
    dq 0                                                        ; null descriptor
.code: equ $ - gdt64
    dq (1 << 43) | (1 << 44) | (1 << 47) | (1 << 53)          ; 64-bit code segment
.pointer:
    dw $ - gdt64 - 1
    dq gdt64

; ─── 32-bit entry point — both MB1 and MB2 land here ──────────────────────────
;
; EAX = bootloader magic (0x2BADB002 or 0x36D76289)
; EBX = physical address of the respective info struct
;
; We preserve both registers verbatim into RDI/RSI which become the first two
; System V AMD64 ABI arguments to kernel_main(uint32_t magic, uint32_t info).
;
section .text
bits 32
global _start
_start:
    mov esp, stack_top
    mov edi, eax            ; arg1 = magic
    mov esi, ebx            ; arg2 = info struct phys addr

    call check_cpuid
    call check_long_mode
    call setup_page_tables
    call enable_paging

    lgdt [gdt64.pointer]
    jmp gdt64.code:long_mode_start

; ── CPUID availability check ──
check_cpuid:
    pushfd
    pop eax
    mov ecx, eax
    xor eax, 1 << 21
    push eax
    popfd
    pushfd
    pop eax
    push ecx
    popfd
    cmp eax, ecx
    je .no_cpuid
    ret
.no_cpuid:
    hlt

; ── Long mode availability check ──
check_long_mode:
    mov eax, 0x80000000
    cpuid
    cmp eax, 0x80000001
    jb .no_long_mode
    mov eax, 0x80000001
    cpuid
    test edx, 1 << 29
    jz .no_long_mode
    ret
.no_long_mode:
    hlt

; ── Identity-map first 1 GiB with 2 MiB pages ──
setup_page_tables:
    mov eax, p3_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p4_table], eax
    mov eax, p2_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table], eax
    mov ecx, 0
.map_p2:
    mov eax, 0x200000
    mul ecx
    or  eax, 0b10000011     ; present + writable + huge
    mov [p2_table + ecx * 8], eax
    inc ecx
    cmp ecx, 512
    jne .map_p2
    ret

; ── Enable PAE, load CR3, set EFER.LME, enable paging ──
enable_paging:
    mov eax, p4_table
    mov cr3, eax
    mov eax, cr4
    or  eax, (1 << 5)       ; PAE
    or  eax, (1 << 9)       ; OSFXSR  (SSE)
    or  eax, (1 << 10)      ; OSXMMEXCPT
    mov cr4, eax
    mov ecx, 0xC0000080     ; EFER MSR
    rdmsr
    or  eax, 1 << 8         ; LME
    wrmsr
    mov eax, cr0
    and eax, ~(1 << 2)      ; clear EM
    or  eax,  (1 << 1)      ; set MP
    or  eax,  (1 << 31)     ; PG
    mov cr0, eax
    ret

; ─── 64-bit long mode ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
; RDI = magic, RSI = info_phys  (System V AMD64 ABI arg1/arg2)
section .text
bits 64
extern kernel_main
long_mode_start:
    mov ax, 0
    mov ss, ax
    mov ds, ax
    mov es, ax
    mov fs, ax
    mov gs, ax

    mov edi, edi            ; zero-extend to 64 bits
    mov esi, esi

    call kernel_main
    cli

input.new_string:
; ── Identity-map first 4 GiB with 2 MiB pages ──
; Covers the kernel at 1 MiB and the UEFI GOP framebuffer (often at 2–3 GiB).
setup_page_tables:
    ; P4[0] → P3
    mov eax, p3_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p4_table], eax

    ; P3[0..3] → p2_table_0..3  (one entry per GiB)
    mov eax, p2_table_0
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 0 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_1
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 1 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_2
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 2 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_3
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 3 * 8], eax

    ; Fill all four PDs: 4 × 512 entries = 2048 × 2 MiB pages = 4 GiB
    mov ecx, 0
.map_p2:
    mov eax, 0x200000       ; 2 MiB per entry
    mul ecx
    or  eax, 0b10000011     ; present + writable + huge

    ; Select the right PD based on which GiB we're in (ecx / 512)
    ; GiB 0: ecx 0–511   → p2_table_0
    ; GiB 1: ecx 512–1023 → p2_table_1
    ; GiB 2: ecx 1024–1535 → p2_table_2
    ; GiB 3: ecx 1536–2047 → p2_table_3
    mov edx, ecx
    and edx, 511            ; index within the PD (0–511)
    cmp ecx, 512
    jb  .gib0
    cmp ecx, 1024
    jb  .gib1
    cmp ecx, 1536
    jb  .gib2
    mov [p2_table_3 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib0:
    mov [p2_table_0 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib1:
    mov [p2_table_1 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib2:
    mov [p2_table_2 + edx * 8], eax
.next:
    inc ecx
    cmp ecx, 2048
    jne .map_p2
    ret

toolUseResult.originalFile:
; ─── Multiboot 2 header ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
;
; Must appear within the first 32 KiB of the OS image, 64-bit aligned.
; On MB2 entry: EAX = 0x36D76289, EBX = physical address of MB2 info struct.
;
MB2_MAGIC       equ 0xE85250D6
MB2_ARCH        equ 0               ; i386 protected mode
MB2_ENTRY_MAGIC equ 0x36D76289

section .mb2hdr
align 8
mb2_header_start:
    dd MB2_MAGIC
    dd MB2_ARCH
    dd (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start)
    dd -(MB2_MAGIC + MB2_ARCH + (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start))

    ; ── Tag: Framebuffer request (type 5) ─────────────────────────────────────
    ; flags=1 → optional (fall back gracefully if firmware can't satisfy it).
    ; width=0 height=0 → no resolution preference; depth=32 → 32 bpp BGRA/BGRX.
    align 8
    dw 5            ; type  = framebuffer
    dw 1            ; flags = optional
    dd 20           ; size  = 20 bytes
    dd 0            ; preferred width  (0 = don't care)
    dd 0            ; preferred height (0 = don't care)
    dd 32           ; preferred depth  (32 bpp)

    ; ── End tag ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    align 8
    dw 0            ; type  = end
    dw 0            ; flags
    dd 8            ; size
mb2_header_end:

; ─── Multiboot 1 header (legacy fallback) ──────────────────────────────────────
;
; Placed immediately after the MB2 header so GRUB (MB1) can still find it within
; the first 8 KiB.  On MB1 entry: EAX = 0x2BADB002, EBX = MB1 info phys addr.
;
MB1_ALIGN     equ 1 << 0
MB1_MEMINFO   equ 1 << 1
MB1_FLAGS     equ MB1_ALIGN | MB1_MEMINFO
MB1_MAGIC     equ 0x1BADB002
MB1_CHECKSUM  equ -(MB1_MAGIC + MB1_FLAGS)

section .mb2hdr          ; same section — keeps both headers contiguous and early
align 4
    dd MB1_MAGIC
    dd MB1_FLAGS
    dd MB1_CHECKSUM

; ─── Stacks + page tables (BSS) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .bss
align 16
stack_bottom:
    resb 16384          ; 16 KiB stack
stack_top:

align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT  (covers 0–512 GiB, one entry per GiB)
p2_table_0: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 0   (0x00000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF)
p2_table_1: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 1   (0x40000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF)
p2_table_2: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 2   (0x80000000 – 0xBFFFFFFF)
p2_table_3: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 3   (0xC0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF)

; ─── GDT for long mode ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .rodata
gdt64:
    dq 0                                                        ; null descriptor
.code: equ $ - gdt64
    dq (1 << 43) | (1 << 44) | (1 << 47) | (1 << 53)          ; 64-bit code segment
.pointer:
    dw $ - gdt64 - 1
    dq gdt64

; ─── 32-bit entry point — both MB1 and MB2 land here ──────────────────────────
;
; EAX = bootloader magic (0x2BADB002 or 0x36D76289)
; EBX = physical address of the respective info struct
;
; We preserve both registers verbatim into RDI/RSI which become the first two
; System V AMD64 ABI arguments to kernel_main(uint32_t magic, uint32_t info).
;
section .text
bits 32
global _start
_start:
    mov esp, stack_top
    mov edi, eax            ; arg1 = magic
    mov esi, ebx            ; arg2 = info struct phys addr

    call check_cpuid
    call check_long_mode
    call setup_page_tables
    call enable_paging

    lgdt [gdt64.pointer]
    jmp gdt64.code:long_mode_start

; ── CPUID availability check ──
check_cpuid:
    pushfd
    pop eax
    mov ecx, eax
    xor eax, 1 << 21
    push eax
    popfd
    pushfd
    pop eax
    push ecx
    popfd
    cmp eax, ecx
    je .no_cpuid
    ret
.no_cpuid:
    hlt

; ── Long mode availability check ──
check_long_mode:
    mov eax, 0x80000000
    cpuid
    cmp eax, 0x80000001
    jb .no_long_mode
    mov eax, 0x80000001
    cpuid
    test edx, 1 << 29
    jz .no_long_mode
    ret
.no_long_mode:
    hlt

; ── Identity-map first 1 GiB with 2 MiB pages ──
setup_page_tables:
    mov eax, p3_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p4_table], eax
    mov eax, p2_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table], eax
    mov ecx, 0
.map_p2:
    mov eax, 0x200000
    mul ecx
    or  eax, 0b10000011     ; present + writable + huge
    mov [p2_table + ecx * 8], eax
    inc ecx
    cmp ecx, 512
    jne .map_p2
    ret

; ── Enable PAE, load CR3, set EFER.LME, enable paging ──
enable_paging:
    mov eax, p4_table
    mov cr3, eax
    mov eax, cr4
    or  eax, (1 << 5)       ; PAE
    or  eax, (1 << 9)       ; OSFXSR  (SSE)
    or  eax, (1 << 10)      ; OSXMMEXCPT
    mov cr4, eax
    mov ecx, 0xC0000080     ; EFER MSR
    rdmsr
    or  eax, 1 << 8         ; LME
    wrmsr
    mov eax, cr0
    and eax, ~(1 << 2)      ; clear EM
    or  eax,  (1 << 1)      ; set MP
    or  eax,  (1 << 31)     ; PG
    mov cr0, eax
    ret

; ─── 64-bit long mode ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
; RDI = magic, RSI = info_phys  (System V AMD64 ABI arg1/arg2)
section .text
bits 64
extern kernel_main
long_mode_start:
    mov ax, 0
    mov ss, ax
    mov ds, ax
    mov es, ax
    mov fs, ax
    mov gs, ax

    mov edi, edi            ; zero-extend to 64 bits
    mov esi, esi

    call kernel_main
    cli
.hang:

    jmp .hang

content.text:
Now rebuild and run:

```bash
make iso && make run-uefi
```

This time `gop_init_from_mb2_tag` will accept the `type=1 bpp=32` tag, use the BGRX layout, and the framebuffer at `0x80000000` will be accessible because all 4 GiB are now mapped.
fix the snake game to properly support both MB2 and MB1
Let me read the snake source first:

The snake game bypasses the kernel's display abstraction entirely — it writes directly to `0xB8000`. On the GOP path that address is just RAM with no display behind it. The fix is to route all drawing through `kprint`-style syscalls, but that would be a complete rewrite of the rendering. The better approach is to expose a display-mode query syscall and have the snake draw through the GOP framebuffer when needed.

Let me check what syscalls exist:

lines: +/* SYS_PUTCELL: draw one character cell at (col, row).
+ * arg3 = (uint16_t)(char | (attr << 8)) — same encoding as VGA text RAM.

content.text:
LSP noise only. Build:

```bash
make iso && make run-uefi
```

**What changed:**

- Removed all `VGA_ADDR[...]` direct writes from snake — it no longer touches `0xB8000`
- All cell drawing goes through `SYS_PUTCELL` → `sys_putcell()` in the kernel, which calls `vga_goto` + `kprint_char` — the same path that already works on both VGA text and GOP
- `SYS_CLEAR` was already routed through `vga_clear()` which is also dual-path
- The MB1 BIOS legacy entry works identically since `vga_init()` sets `use_gop_path = false` and everything falls through to `0xB8000` writes inside the kernel
for MB2 we can afford better graphics and more screen space 🙏💀

content.text:
**What the GOP snake now does differently from the VGA version:**

- **Board**: dynamically sized from the framebuffer — at 1280×800 that's roughly 54×36 cells at 20px each, vs the old 50×20 char grid
- **Colors**: full 24-bit RGB — dark blue background, bright green head, medium green body, vivid red apple, subtle grid lines, lavender sidebar text
- **Sidebar**: pixel-positioned HUD panel on the right with title, live score/best, controls
- **Pause**: renders an overlay message box over the board rather than clearing the screen
- **Game over**: centered message box with score and best, then clears on keypress
- **Speed**: starts at 10 ticks (slightly faster than VGA's 12), scales down to 4 at score 50

The VGA path is completely unchanged — `snake_main()` queries `SYS_FB_INFO` and dispatches accordingly.

a. the text is fundamentally broken
b. allow users to switch between two color schemes (Green Snake/Red Fruit, Blue Snake/Purple Fruit)
Let me see what the current output looks like and diagnose the text issue first.

The core issue is `sys_putcell` in the kernel: it saves/restores `vga_col`/`vga_row` but calls `vga_goto(col, row)` which on the GOP path calls `kprint_char` which advances the cursor — and also the `gop_render_char` renders at the cursor position *after* `vga_goto` moves it, but `vga_goto` clamps to 80×25. The GOP screen is much wider but `VGA_WIDTH`/`VGA_HEIGHT` are hardcoded as the grid bounds. Let me verify:

I can see both problems clearly now:

**Text bug:** `vga_goto` clamps to `VGA_WIDTH=80, VGA_HEIGHT=25` even on the GOP path — the snake tries to render text at col 140+ and it gets clamped to col 79. Also `sys_putcell` saves/restores the cursor wrong — it calls `vga_goto` then `kprint_char` which *advances* the cursor before the restore happens, leaving the global cursor corrupted between cells.

**Fix:** `sys_putcell` on GOP should write directly to the pixel framebuffer at `col*8, row*16` without touching the global cursor at all. And `vga_goto` needs to use the actual GOP grid dimensions, not the hardcoded VGA ones.

I'll fix the kernel side properly, then do the colour scheme switcher in snake:

aiTitle: Upgrade code to Multiboot 2 for UEFI and GOP graphics

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - **Initial**: Upgrade NephilimOS from Multiboot 1 to Multiboot 2 to enable UEFI boot and GOP (Graphics Output Protocol) framebuffer graphics.
   - **Refinement 1**: Keep MB1 as a legacy fallback (two grub.cfg entries, dual header in boot.asm).
   - **Refinement 2**: GOP framebuffer driver must be in a separate file (`kernel/gop.c`); three-tier fallback: MB2+GOP → MB2+VGA text → MB1+VGA text.
   - **Snake fix**: Fix snake game to properly support both MB2 (pixel renderer) and MB1 (ASCII text).
   - **GOP snake enhancement**: Use better graphics and more screen space on the MB2/GOP path.
   - **Current**: (a) Fix fundamentally broken text rendering on GOP path; (b) Add two switchable colour schemes to the GOP snake renderer (Green snake/Red fruit vs Blue snake/Purple fruit), switchable with `C` key.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Multiboot 2 specification: header format (magic 0xE85250D6), tag stream, framebuffer tag (type 8), end tag (type 0), 8-byte alignment
   - Multiboot 1 legacy: magic 0x1BADB002, MB1 info struct
   - UEFI GOP: framebuffer provided via MB2 tag type 8; `framebuffer_type` field (1=indexed, 2=direct); GRUB requires `insmod all_video` + `set gfxpayload=keep` before `multiboot2`
   - QEMU virtio-gpu quirk: reports `framebuffer_type=1` (indexed) despite being a 32-bpp BGRX direct-colour buffer at 0x80000000
   - x86_64 identity paging: expanded from 1 GiB to 4 GiB (four P2 tables) to cover framebuffer at 0x80000000
   - OVMF split images: `ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin` (readonly pflash) + `ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin` (writable, copied to /tmp)
   - VGA text mode: 0xB8000, 80×25 char grid, 8×16 font, attribute bytes
   - GOP pixel rendering: linear framebuffer, pitch-based row addressing, 8×16 font blitting
   - Dual display path: `use_gop_path` bool in vga.c; `grid_cols()`/`grid_rows()` dynamically return fb_width/8 or 80
   - `sys_putcell` design: must write directly to framebuffer/VGA RAM without touching global cursor state
   - Syscall convention: int 0x80, args in rdi/rsi/rdx/rcx/r8, retrieved from `int_frame_t`
   - VGA 16-colour palette translated to 24-bit RGB for GOP rendering

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **boot/boot.asm** — Complete rewrite. Contains both MB2 and MB1 headers in `.mb2hdr` section. Expanded page tables to 4 GiB.
     ```nasm
     ; MB2 header + framebuffer tag (type 5, optional, 32bpp)
     ; MB1 header immediately after
     ; BSS: p4_table, p3_table, p2_table_0..3 (each 4096 bytes)
     ; P3[0..3] → p2_table_0..3; each PD maps 512×2MiB = 1GiB; total 4GiB
     ```

   - **linker.ld** — Changed `.multiboot` → `.mb2hdr` section name.

   - **grub/grub.cfg** — Two entries:
     ```
     menuentry "NephilimOS (Multiboot 2 / UEFI)" {
         insmod all_video
         set gfxpayload=keep
         multiboot2 /boot/nephilim.kernel
         boot
     }
     menuentry "NephilimOS (Multiboot 1 / BIOS legacy)" {
         terminal_output console
         multiboot /boot/nephilim.kernel
         boot
     }
     ```

   - **include/gop.h** — New file. Defines `gop_info_t`, `gop_mb2_tag_t`, `gop_available`, drawing API, inline `gop_pack_color()`.

   - **kernel/gop.c** — New file. `gop_init_from_mb2_tag()` accepts type=1 or type=2 with ≥15 bpp:
     ```c
     if (tag->framebuffer_type == 2) {
         /* use tag's channel positions */
     } else {
         /* BGRX hardcode: red=16, green=8, blue=0 */
         gop_fb.red_pos=16; gop_fb.green_pos=8; gop_fb.blue_pos=0;
     }
     ```

   - **include/pmm.h** — Replaced MB1-only struct with both MB2 tag structs and MB1 structs. API: `pmm_init_mb2(uint32_t)` + `pmm_init_mb1(uint32_t)`.

   - **kernel/pmm.c** — Two init paths + shared `pmm_common_finalize()`.

   - **kernel/vga.c** — Critical fix for broken text:
     ```c
     static inline size_t grid_cols(void) {
         return use_gop_path ? (size_t)(gop_fb.width / GOP_CELL_W) : (size_t)VGA_WIDTH;
     }
     static inline size_t grid_rows(void) {
         return use_gop_path ? (size_t)(gop_fb.height / GOP_CELL_H) : (size_t)VGA_HEIGHT;
     }
     void vga_goto(size_t col, size_t row) {
         size_t gc = grid_cols(), gr = grid_rows();
         if (col >= gc) col = gc - 1;
         if (row >= gr) row = gr - 1;
         vga_col = col; vga_row = row;
         if (!use_gop_path) vga_cursor_move(col, row);
     }
     ```
     Also `vga_putchar` and `vga_scroll` updated to use `grid_cols()`/`grid_rows()`.

   - **include/vga.h** — Added `vga_init_gop()` declaration, `<stdbool.h>`.

   - **kernel/kernel.c** — Three-tier boot, `mb2_find_tag()`, serial debug prints, calls `pmm_init_mb2/mb1`.

   - **include/syscall.h** — Added SYS_DISPLAY_MODE(36), SYS_PUTCELL(37), SYS_SETCURSOR(38), SYS_FB_INFO(39), SYS_FB_FILLRECT(40); `fb_info_t` struct; `syscall4()`, `syscall5()` helpers.

   - **kernel/syscall.c** — Added `#include <gop.h>`, `arg4=frame->rcx`, `arg5=frame->r8`. Critical `sys_putcell` rewrite:
     ```c
     static int64_t sys_putcell(uint64_t col, uint64_t row, uint64_t packed) {
         char ch = (char)(packed & 0xFF);
         uint8_t attr = (uint8_t)((packed >> 8) & 0xFF);
         size_t sc=vga_col, sr=vga_row; uint8_t sa=vga_color;
         vga_color=attr; vga_col=(size_t)col; vga_row=(size_t)row;
         if (gop_available) {
             /* inline palette → GOP colour, call gop_draw_glyph(col*8, row*16, ...) */
         } else {
             volatile uint16_t *vram = (volatile uint16_t*)0xB8000;
             vram[row*80+col] = (uint16_t)(uint8_t)ch | ((uint16_t)attr<<8);
         }
         vga_col=sc; vga_row=sr; vga_color=sa;
         return ESYS_OK;
     }
     ```

   - **userland/snake/main.c** — Complete dual-renderer rewrite:
     - VGA path: `snake_vga()` using SYS_PUTCELL, unchanged game logic
     - GOP path: `snake_gop(fb_info_t*)` with pixel renderer
     - Colour schemes:
       ```c
       typedef struct { uint32_t bg,border,grid,head,body,fruit,sidebar;
                        uint8_t title_attr,score_attr,best_attr; } color_scheme_t;
       static const color_scheme_t schemes[2] = {
           { /* Green/Red */ .head=RGB(0x00,0xFF,0x88), .body=RGB(0x00,0xCC,0x44),
             .fruit=RGB(0xFF,0x22,0x33), ... },
           { /* Blue/Purple */ .head=RGB(0x44,0xAA,0xFF), .body=RGB(0x22,0x66,0xCC),
             .fruit=RGB(0xCC,0x44,0xFF), ... },
       };
       #define S (schemes[g_scheme])
       ```
     - `C` key cycles `g_scheme`, calls `gop_repaint_all()`
     - `fill()` → `syscall5(SYS_FB_FILLRECT,...)`
     - `snake_main()` queries `SYS_FB_INFO`, dispatches to `snake_gop` or `snake_vga`

   - **Makefile** — Added `kernel/gop.o`, GRUB_MODULES with `all_video`, `run-uefi` target with split OVMF pflash drives + /tmp vars copy.

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **grub-mkrescue: cannot open efi_gop.mod**: `efi_gop` only in x86_64-efi GRUB tree. Fix: removed `efi_gop efi_uga` from GRUB_MODULES.
   - **OVMF.fd not found / "could not load PC BIOS"**: openSUSE uses split code+vars images. Fix: installed `qemu-ovmf-x86_64`, switched to `-drive if=pflash` with `ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin` + `ovmf-x86_64-4m-vars.bin`; vars copied to `/tmp/nephilim-ovmf-vars.bin`.
   - **"no suitable video mode found"**: `gfxterm` tries to init video on load. Fix: removed `gfxterm` from GRUB_MODULES.
   - **"WARNING: no console will be available to OS"**: `all_video` not loaded. Fix: `insmod all_video` in MB2 entry per OSDev wiki; added `all_video` to GRUB_MODULES; `set gfxpayload=keep`.
   - **GOP init FAILED (type=1)**: QEMU virtio-gpu reports framebuffer_type=1 despite being 32bpp BGRX. Serial debug revealed `type=1 bpp=32`. Fix: `gop_init_from_mb2_tag()` now accepts type≠2 with ≥15bpp, hardcodes BGRX layout.
   - **Framebuffer at 0x80000000 inaccessible**: Only 1 GiB mapped. Fix: added `p2_table_1/2/3` to BSS, `setup_page_tables` maps 4 GiB (2048 entries).
   - **MB1 "no console" warning**: `all_video` on BIOS path breaks text console. Fix: `terminal_output console` in MB1 entry.
   - **Text fundamentally broken on GOP**: (a) `sys_putcell` was calling `vga_goto()+kprint_char()` which advanced global cursor, corrupting state; (b) `vga_goto` clamped to hardcoded 80×25 even on GOP where grid is ~160×50. Fix: added `grid_cols()`/`grid_rows()` helpers; rewrote `sys_putcell` to write directly without touching cursor; updated all wrap/scroll to use dynamic grid dims.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Successfully established MB2+GOP boot path through OVMF on QEMU
   - Diagnosed GOP framebuffer type quirk via serial debug output (`[BOOT] fb tag: addr=0x80000000 w=1280 h=800 bpp=32 type=1`)
   - Resolved text corruption by separating "draw at position" from "advance cursor" concerns in sys_putcell
   - Dual-renderer snake preserves full MB1 ASCII experience while adding rich pixel graphics on MB2/UEFI

6. All user messages:
   - "upgrade my code to multiboot 2 so I can use UEFI and in turn GOP for Graphics"
   - "also let the older ones remain for legacy fallback"
   - "have fallback for both MB2 and for GOP based framebuffer, also for GOB framebuffer create a separate file"
   - [Paste of grub-mkrescue efi_gop.mod error]
   - [Paste of make run-uefi OVMF path errors and zypper install output]
   - [Paste of find /usr/share/qemu output showing ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin etc.]
   - [Screenshot: "error: no suitable video mode found"]
   - "fix this" (referring to video mode error)
   - [Screenshot: "WARNING: no console will be available to OS"]
   - "well it now shows this for MB1 and for MB2 no actual text is rendered just plain black screen"
   - [Paste of OSDev GOP wiki article — key point: `insmod all_video` required before multiboot2]
   - [Paste of serial output showing successful MB2 boot but GOP init FAILED with type=1]
   - "fix the snake game to properly support both MB2 and MB1"
   - "for MB2 we can afford better graphics and more screen space 🙏💀"
   - "a. the text is fundamentally broken / b. allow users to switch between two color schemes (Green Snake/Red Fruit, Blue Snake/Purple Fruit)"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Build and test after the text fix + colour scheme changes: `make iso && make run-uefi`
   - The colour scheme switcher (`C` key) and all scheme-aware rendering is coded but not yet tested

8. Current Work:
   Working on two simultaneous fixes to the GOP snake renderer:
   
   **(a) Text fix** — `vga.c` and `syscall.c`:
   - Added `grid_cols()`/`grid_rows()` to vga.c that return framebuffer-sized grid on GOP
   - Updated `vga_goto`, `vga_putchar`, `vga_scroll` to use dynamic grid dimensions
   - Rewrote `sys_putcell` to write directly at `col*8, row*16` in pixels (GOP) or `row*80+col` (VGA) without touching the global cursor

   **(b) Colour schemes** — `userland/snake/main.c`:
   - Defined `color_scheme_t` struct and `schemes[2]` array (Green/Red and Blue/Purple)
   - `g_scheme` int + `S` macro
   - All GOP render functions (`gop_draw_grid`, `gop_draw_sidebar`, `gop_draw_border`, `gop_draw_snake`, `gop_step`, etc.) updated to use `S.field`
   - `C` key in game loop: cycles scheme, calls `gop_repaint_all()`
   - `gop_repaint_all()` does full screen redraw with new scheme colours
   - Sidebar shows "C  colors" hint and current scheme indicator

   Last edit was: `fill(0, 0, G_fbw, G_fbh, S.bg)` in the end-game cleanup section of `snake_gop()`.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Build and test the changes:
   ```bash
   make iso && make run-uefi

file.content:
; ─── Multiboot 2 header ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
;
; Must appear within the first 32 KiB of the OS image, 64-bit aligned.
; On MB2 entry: EAX = 0x36D76289, EBX = physical address of MB2 info struct.
;
MB2_MAGIC       equ 0xE85250D6
MB2_ARCH        equ 0               ; i386 protected mode
MB2_ENTRY_MAGIC equ 0x36D76289

section .mb2hdr
align 8
mb2_header_start:
    dd MB2_MAGIC
    dd MB2_ARCH
    dd (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start)
    dd -(MB2_MAGIC + MB2_ARCH + (mb2_header_end - mb2_header_start))

    ; ── Tag: Framebuffer request (type 5) ─────────────────────────────────────
    ; flags=1 → optional (fall back gracefully if firmware can't satisfy it).
    ; width=0 height=0 → no resolution preference; depth=32 → 32 bpp BGRA/BGRX.
    align 8
    dw 5            ; type  = framebuffer
    dw 1            ; flags = optional
    dd 20           ; size  = 20 bytes
    dd 0            ; preferred width  (0 = don't care)
    dd 0            ; preferred height (0 = don't care)
    dd 32           ; preferred depth  (32 bpp)

    ; ── End tag ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    align 8
    dw 0            ; type  = end
    dw 0            ; flags
    dd 8            ; size
mb2_header_end:

; ─── Multiboot 1 header (legacy fallback) ──────────────────────────────────────
;
; Placed immediately after the MB2 header so GRUB (MB1) can still find it within
; the first 8 KiB.  On MB1 entry: EAX = 0x2BADB002, EBX = MB1 info phys addr.
;
MB1_ALIGN     equ 1 << 0
MB1_MEMINFO   equ 1 << 1
MB1_FLAGS     equ MB1_ALIGN | MB1_MEMINFO
MB1_MAGIC     equ 0x1BADB002
MB1_CHECKSUM  equ -(MB1_MAGIC + MB1_FLAGS)

section .mb2hdr          ; same section — keeps both headers contiguous and early
align 4
    dd MB1_MAGIC
    dd MB1_FLAGS
    dd MB1_CHECKSUM

; ─── Stacks + page tables (BSS) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .bss
align 16
stack_bottom:
    resb 16384          ; 16 KiB stack
stack_top:

align 4096
p4_table:   resb 4096   ; PML4
p3_table:   resb 4096   ; PDPT  (covers 0–512 GiB, one entry per GiB)
p2_table_0: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 0   (0x00000000 – 0x3FFFFFFF)
p2_table_1: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 1   (0x40000000 – 0x7FFFFFFF)
p2_table_2: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 2   (0x80000000 – 0xBFFFFFFF)
p2_table_3: resb 4096   ; PD for GiB 3   (0xC0000000 – 0xFFFFFFFF)

; ─── GDT for long mode ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
section .rodata
gdt64:
    dq 0                                                        ; null descriptor
.code: equ $ - gdt64
    dq (1 << 43) | (1 << 44) | (1 << 47) | (1 << 53)          ; 64-bit code segment
.pointer:
    dw $ - gdt64 - 1
    dq gdt64

; ─── 32-bit entry point — both MB1 and MB2 land here ──────────────────────────
;
; EAX = bootloader magic (0x2BADB002 or 0x36D76289)
; EBX = physical address of the respective info struct
;
; We preserve both registers verbatim into RDI/RSI which become the first two
; System V AMD64 ABI arguments to kernel_main(uint32_t magic, uint32_t info).
;
section .text
bits 32
global _start
_start:
    mov esp, stack_top
    mov edi, eax            ; arg1 = magic
    mov esi, ebx            ; arg2 = info struct phys addr

    call check_cpuid
    call check_long_mode
    call setup_page_tables
    call enable_paging

    lgdt [gdt64.pointer]
    jmp gdt64.code:long_mode_start

; ── CPUID availability check ──
check_cpuid:
    pushfd
    pop eax
    mov ecx, eax
    xor eax, 1 << 21
    push eax
    popfd
    pushfd
    pop eax
    push ecx
    popfd
    cmp eax, ecx
    je .no_cpuid
    ret
.no_cpuid:
    hlt

; ── Long mode availability check ──
check_long_mode:
    mov eax, 0x80000000
    cpuid
    cmp eax, 0x80000001
    jb .no_long_mode
    mov eax, 0x80000001
    cpuid
    test edx, 1 << 29
    jz .no_long_mode
    ret
.no_long_mode:
    hlt

; ── Identity-map first 4 GiB with 2 MiB pages ──
; Covers the kernel at 1 MiB and the UEFI GOP framebuffer (often at 2–3 GiB).
setup_page_tables:
    ; P4[0] → P3
    mov eax, p3_table
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p4_table], eax

    ; P3[0..3] → p2_table_0..3  (one entry per GiB)
    mov eax, p2_table_0
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 0 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_1
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 1 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_2
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 2 * 8], eax

    mov eax, p2_table_3
    or  eax, 0b11
    mov [p3_table + 3 * 8], eax

    ; Fill all four PDs: 4 × 512 entries = 2048 × 2 MiB pages = 4 GiB
    mov ecx, 0
.map_p2:
    mov eax, 0x200000       ; 2 MiB per entry
    mul ecx
    or  eax, 0b10000011     ; present + writable + huge

    ; Select the right PD based on which GiB we're in (ecx / 512)
    ; GiB 0: ecx 0–511   → p2_table_0
    ; GiB 1: ecx 512–1023 → p2_table_1
    ; GiB 2: ecx 1024–1535 → p2_table_2
    ; GiB 3: ecx 1536–2047 → p2_table_3
    mov edx, ecx
    and edx, 511            ; index within the PD (0–511)
    cmp ecx, 512
    jb  .gib0
    cmp ecx, 1024
    jb  .gib1
    cmp ecx, 1536
    jb  .gib2
    mov [p2_table_3 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib0:
    mov [p2_table_0 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib1:
    mov [p2_table_1 + edx * 8], eax
    jmp .next
.gib2:
    mov [p2_table_2 + edx * 8], eax
.next:
    inc ecx
    cmp ecx, 2048
    jne .map_p2
    ret

; ── Enable PAE, load CR3, set EFER.LME, enable paging ──
enable_paging:
    mov eax, p4_table
    mov cr3, eax
    mov eax, cr4
    or  eax, (1 << 5)       ; PAE
    or  eax, (1 << 9)       ; OSFXSR  (SSE)
    or  eax, (1 << 10)      ; OSXMMEXCPT
    mov cr4, eax
    mov ecx, 0xC0000080     ; EFER MSR
    rdmsr
    or  eax, 1 << 8         ; LME
    wrmsr
    mov eax, cr0
    and eax, ~(1 << 2)      ; clear EM
    or  eax,  (1 << 1)      ; set MP
    or  eax,  (1 << 31)     ; PG
    mov cr0, eax
    ret

; ─── 64-bit long mode ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
; RDI = magic, RSI = info_phys  (System V AMD64 ABI arg1/arg2)
section .text
bits 64
extern kernel_main
long_mode_start:
    mov ax, 0
    mov ss, ax
    mov ds, ax
    mov es, ax
    mov fs, ax
    mov gs, ax

    mov edi, edi            ; zero-extend to 64 bits
    mov esi, esi

    call kernel_main
    cli
.hang:
    hlt
    jmp .hang

file.content:
GRUB_MKRESCUE := $(shell which grub2-mkrescue 2>/dev/null || which grub-mkrescue 2>/dev/null)

# grub-mkrescue flags:
#   --modules includes both multiboot (MB1) and multiboot2 (MB2/UEFI) modules
#   plus efi_gop so GRUB can negotiate a GOP framebuffer on UEFI firmware.
GRUB_MODULES := multiboot multiboot2 normal iso9660 all_video

iso: $(TARGET)
	mkdir -p $(ISODIR)/boot/grub
	cp $(TARGET) $(ISODIR)/boot/$(TARGET)
	cp grub/grub.cfg $(ISODIR)/boot/grub/grub.cfg
	$(GRUB_MKRESCUE) --modules="$(GRUB_MODULES)" -o $(ISO) $(ISODIR)

# ── Run ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
QEMU    := $(shell which qemu-system-x86_64 2>/dev/null)
# OVMF firmware paths — openSUSE ships split code+vars images in qemu-ovmf-x86_64.
# Override on the command line if your distro puts them elsewhere:
#   make run-uefi OVMF_CODE=/path/to/code.fd OVMF_VARS=/path/to/vars.fd
OVMF_CODE ?= $(firstword \
    $(wildcard /usr/share/qemu/ovmf-x86_64-4m-code.bin) \

content.text: The issue is `sys_shutdown` uses QEMU ACPI ports (`0x604`, `0xB004`) which only work under QEMU/BIOS. Under UEFI/MB2, ACPI shutdown needs to go through the ACPI reset register or UEFI runtime services — but since we don't have UEFI runtime services after ExitBootServices, the practical approach is to also try the ACPI PM1a control register. Let me check if there's a way to detect the boot mode and use the right method.

Good — `gop_available` is the flag for MB2/UEFI path. The real fix for shutdown under UEFI is to use the ACPI reset register (port `0xCF9`, value `0x06` for reset) and for shutdown to try the QEMU fw_cfg / ACPI sleep via the standard ACPI PM1 control block. Since we don't parse ACPI tables, the practical portable approach is:

- **Reboot**: port `0xCF9` with `0x06` works on both BIOS and UEFI QEMU
- **Shutdown**: QEMU UEFI uses port `0x604` just like BIOS — both ports already in the code. The real issue is that the *shell help text* doesn't mention a difference, and there's no instruction shown to the user anywhere in MB2 mode about how to shut down.

Let me check what the shell actually prints and where any boot instructions are displayed:

content.text:
Now I understand the full picture. The user's question is about updating the `shutdown`/`reboot` commands to actually work under MB2/UEFI. The existing `sys_shutdown` tries `0x604` and `0xB004` (QEMU ACPI), and `sys_reboot` uses the PS/2 reset line (`0x64`/`0xFE`). Under UEFI QEMU these may or may not work. Let me make both more robust:

- **Reboot**: add port `0xCF9` (`0x06`) first — the ACPI/PCIe reset register, works on both BIOS and UEFI QEMU
- **Shutdown**: `0x604` already works with QEMU even under UEFI; add `0x2000` to port `0x4004` (QEMU PIIX4 fallback) and a final `hlt` loop

Clean. Here's what changed:

**Reboot** — now tries `0xCF9` (ACPI/PCIe hard reset register) first, which works on both BIOS and UEFI QEMU, then falls back to the PS/2 controller pulse.

**Shutdown** — already worked on UEFI QEMU via `0x604`; added the microvm/virt fallback at `0x4004`. The `cli; hlt` loop at the end ensures the CPU halts with interrupts disabled if all ports are ignored.

file.content:
TARGET   := nephilim.kernel
ISO      := nephilim.iso
ISODIR   := isodir

CC       := clang
LD       := ld.lld
NASM     := nasm
PYTHON   := $(shell which python 2>/dev/null || which python3 2>/dev/null)

INCLUDES := -Iinclude

# Kernel flags: target is x86-64 — SSE/AVX available and enabled
CFLAGS   := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP
LDFLAGS  := -m elf_x86_64 -nostdlib -T linker.ld \
            --no-dynamic-linker -z max-page-size=0x1000

# Userland flags
UCFLAGS  := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 -Wall -Wextra \
            $(INCLUDES) -MMD -MP

# MuJS flags — relax warnings for third-party code
JSCFLAGS := --target=x86_64-elf -std=gnu99 -ffreestanding -O2 \
            -mno-red-zone -mcmodel=kernel \
            $(INCLUDES) -Iexternal/include/mujs \
            -Wno-unused-function -Wno-unused-variable \
            -Wno-sign-compare -Wno-implicit-fallthrough \
            -Wno-missing-field-initializers

# ── Kernel objects ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KOBJS    := boot/boot.o boot/gdt_flush.o boot/isr_stubs.o boot/ctx_switch.o \
            boot/font8x16.o \
            drivers/keyboard.o drivers/serial.o \
            kernel/kernel.o kernel/vga.o kernel/gop.o kernel/detect.o \
            kernel/gdt.o kernel/idt.o kernel/syscall.o kernel/process.o \
            kernel/timer.o kernel/pmm.o kernel/vmm.o \
            kernel/js_runtime.o kernel/seed.o kernel/minit.o \
            fs/vfs.o fs/rawfs.o fs/ext2.o \
            lib/libtranslateunix.o \
            stdlib/string.o stdlib/stdlib.o stdlib/malloc.o \
            stdlib/math.o stdlib/printf.o stdlib/setjmp.o stdlib/time.o \
            userland/shell/main.o \
            userland/snake/main.o \
            userland/apps.o

# Auto-generated dependency files (produced by -MMD -MP)
-include $(KOBJS:.o=.d)

.PHONY: all iso clean

all: $(TARGET)

# ── JS source → C string-literal header ──────────────────────────────────────
JS_HEADERS := \
    userland/signal.js.h \
    userland/calculator/main.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/ls.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/cat.js.h \
    userland/coreutils/touch.js.h \

content.text:
Good — I have a full picture now. Let me plan the design before writing:

- **`drivers/voicebox.c`** + **`include/voicebox.h`** — the driver layer with hardware probe + unified API
- Four backends: SB16, AC97, ISA speaker (PIT channel 2), HDA (stub)
- VoiceBox is a **kernel-side driver**, not a userland process — it probes at boot and exposes `vb_beep(freq_hz, ms)` / `vb_play_tone(freq, ms)` to the kernel and via syscall
- minit spawns a **`voicebox_main`** supervisor process that just initialises and idles (so minit can restart it if it crashes)
- New syscall `SYS_BEEP` exposed to userland

/* ── Display syscalls ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
#define SYS_DISPLAY_MODE 36  /* — → 0=VGA text, 1=GOP framebuffer              */
#define SYS_PUTCELL      37  /* rdi=col, rsi=row, rdx=packed(char|attr<<8)      */
#define SYS_SETCURSOR    38  /* rdi=col, rsi=row                                */
#define SYS_FB_INFO      39  /* rdi=fb_info_t* → fills struct, returns 0        */
#define SYS_FB_FILLRECT  40  /* rdi=x,rsi=y,rdx=w, rcx=h, r8=rgb32             */

#define SYSCALL_COUNT 41

/* ── fb_info_t: returned by SYS_FB_INFO ──────────────────────────────────── */
typedef struct {
    uint32_t fb_width;    /* framebuffer width in pixels  (0 if VGA text mode) */
    uint32_t fb_height;   /* framebuffer height in pixels (0 if VGA text mode) */
    uint32_t char_cols;   /* character grid width  (80 for VGA, fb_w/8 for GOP)*/
